rationale: Both round-1 items are ADDRESSED, and I confirmed both empirically (extracted the validator functions verbatim from commit 81784f56 and ran them against the exact adversarial inputs from round 1). (1) validatePathElement now adds a `/\s/.test(value)` whitespace rejection, applied to slug, spec, bead_id (via validateBeadId), and mix[].family; validateArgs now also runs validateShellSafe on slug and spec (bead_id already had it). slug='foo --target evil' now throws 'must not contain whitespace' before any command is built; slug='foo;rm' and bead_id='x|y' throw 'contains a shell metacharacter'; a backtick in spec is caught too (the SHELL_METACHAR_RE backtick is now written as \x60 with a comment explaining this avoids confusing a naive source-scanning test's template-literal tracking — functionally identical, and I confirmed it still matches a literal backtick). The arg-smuggling vector I found in round 1 is closed. (2) validateMix now enum-checks entry.family against VALID_MIX_FAMILIES = {claude, codex} inside validateArgs, so mix=[{family:'gpt',count:1}] throws 'invalid mix[].family: must be one of "claude" or "codex"' at validateArgs time, before Step 2's `panel create` runs -- confirmed by direct call. A clean baseline case still passes with no throw, so the new checks aren't over-broad for legitimate inputs. No new issues found in the delta.
findings: validatePathElement's new whitespace rejection (plugins/mindspec/workflows/ms-panel.js) plus validateShellSafe now applied to slug/spec close the flag-smuggling vector from round 1 -- verified empirically against slug='foo --target evil', slug='foo;rm', bead_id='x --spec evil', bead_id='x|y', spec with an embedded backtick: all throw before validateArgs returns, i.e. before Step 2.
validateMix's new VALID_MIX_FAMILIES enum check rejects a path-clean-but-unrecognized family (e.g. 'gpt') inside validateArgs itself, before Step 2's panel create -- verified empirically.
A clean baseline args object (slug/spec/target all simple tokens, family='claude') still validates with no throw, so the hardening doesn't regress the legitimate-input path.
